metadata.title: Public opinion about Finland joining NATO: analysing Twitter posts by performing natural language processing

abstract: The announcement of Finland's decision to join NATO in May 2022 has provoked many public discussions and debates both inside and outside Finland. The aim of this study is to process natural language in Twitter data collected between 12 May and 3 April 2023 and to analyse the public sentiment on Finland's decision to join NATO. A total of 28,993 tweets were collected and analysed using the VADER algorithm for sentiment analysis and the LDA algorithm for topic modelling. The results show that overall sentiment towards Finland's NATO membership was mostly positive. Topic modelling revealed that the most prevalent topics of the tweets were related to Finland's alliances, concerns, and general opinions of the NATO membership. The results suggest that the public discussion on Twitter focuses on the potential benefits and risks of Finland's decision to join NATO, especially considering the recent tensions with Russia. The study provides insights into public opinion about Finland's NATO membership and highlights the potential of natural language processing techniques for the analysis of social media data. These findings could help in further policy discussions about Finland joining NATO and provide a basis for further research into public opinion on NATO and other international organizations.

paragraphs: In light of the altered security landscape in Europe following Russia's annexation of Crimea in 2014 and the subsequent conflict in eastern Ukraine, certain countries have indicated a desire to join NATO to enhance their security and mitigate potential threats of Russian aggression (Marten 2018). Finland in particular has been the subject of a lot of debate on the question of NATO membership (Forsberg and Vaahtoranta 2001). Despite its neutrality during the Cold War and the years after, Finland has been closer to the alliance in recent years (Alberque and Schreer 2022a;Gunter 2022), and following the Russian invasion of Ukraine in 2022, the Finnish Government expressed its interest in joining NATO 'without delay'. This action was followed by Finland and Sweden's joint application for NATO membership on 18 May 2022 (Alberque and Schreer 2022a;Forsberg 2023). While the negotiations for membership should only take a few months, the process has been delayed due to the opposition of Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdogan, who raised concerns about the presence of suspected terrorist groups on Finnish and Swedish soil (Gunter 2022). Despite this obstacle, Finland finally obtained the approval of all NATO member states, including Turkey, and officially became a member of the alliance on 4 April 2023 (North Atlantic Treaty Organization 2023).

Historically, Finland has maintained neutral policies since the end of the Second World War, preserving independence and avoiding the aggression of the Soviet Union (Jakobson 1969). However, after the collapse of the Soviet Union, Finland re-evaluated its security posture and has since gradually strengthened its cooperation with NATO through various programmes (Raunio and Tiilikainen 2003). With the development of the regional security environment and the balance of power in Europe, Finnish policy makers had been considering NATO membership to strengthen security guarantees and prevent potential threats (Nygren 2008), and following Putin's completely unprovoked invasion of Ukraine on 24 February 2022, Finland and Sweden abandoned traditional military non-alignment policies and started actively seeking NATO membership (Arter 2023).

Finland joining NATO may have a major impact on regional security, European policy, and the future of the alliance. On the one hand, the inclusion of Finland could strengthen NATO's presence in Northern Europe and improve its capacity to address potential security challenges (Michta and Hilde 2016). On the other hand, it may worsen tensions with Russia and undermine the delicate balance of power in the region (Pagung 2019). The debate on Finland's potential accession to NATO involved a variety of stakeholders, including policy makers, academics and the public. Supporters believed that NATO would strengthen Finland's security and prevent potential aggressors (Sloan 2012), while opponents believed that NATO would cause tensions with Russia, undermining Finland's neutrality and necessitating an increase in defence spending (Arter 2023). It is essential to assess public opinion on this issue in order to understand its potential impact on domestic politics and policymaking (Smith 2005).

The decision to seek NATO membership has led to significant public debate in Finland and abroad. This debate is reflected in social media platforms such as Twitter, where users have expressed their views on the subject. Social media platforms are a valuable data source because of their over 4.7 billion users worldwide (Kemp 2023). Further, researchers have used social media communication from platforms like Facebook (Carvajal et al. 2022), Twitter (Stracqualursi, Agati, and Sasahara 2022;Yantseva 2020), Instagram (Lalancette and Raynauld 2019), etc. about political, commercial, and healthcare issues. Further, Finland's public opinion about NATO has suddenly changed dramatically. Since 1998, the Finnish Business Association (EVA) has regularly conducted opinion polls to obtain answers to the same statement that Finland should join NATO. There are five categories that can be chosen: (5) completely disagree; (4) disagree to an extent; (3) difficult to say;

(2) agree to an extent; (1) completely agree. In a time span over 20 years, only a maximum of 13% (1998) and a minimum of 4% (2012) 'completely agreed' that Finland should join NATO. The proportion of people who have completely agreed or agreed to an extent never exceeded 28% (EVA 2021). On the other hand, according to a poll conducted by the Finnish national broadcaster YLE on 28 February 2022, four days after Putin's invasion of Ukraine, 56% of Finns supported NATO membership, and on 9 May, five days before the official application, this figure rose to 76%, with only 12% opposing and 11% unable to say anything (Yle 2022). Between the autumn of 2021 and 28 February 2022, the proportion of NATO supporters doubled and almost tripled on 9 May.

The present study aims to analyse the public opinion about Finland's NATO membership by performing Natural Language Processing (NLP) on Twitter posts that have been collected for this purpose. This method is used to examine the main topics and sentiments of the communication from the public on Twitter concerning Finland's NATO membership. Previous research mainly focused on the implications and possibilities of Finland's accession to NATO, paying particular attention to the impact of regional security dynamics (Arter 1996). The Russian invasion of Ukraine intensified these discussions (Alberque and Schreer 2022a), as researchers began to explore the potential application of Finland's NATO membership and examine the likelihood of such an event occurring in the near future (Alberque and Schreer 2022b). Currently, only one study has focused on the public opinion of Finland joining NATO. Rahkonen (2017) analysed opinion polls in Finland and how NATO topics are covered there by the media. More specifically, this paper aims to answer three key research questions based on the data collected between the announcement of Finland's intend to join NATO 12 May 2022 and the official membership 3 April 2023:

(1) What sentiment has been most prevalent with the public regarding Finland joining NATO? This question will be answered through a sentiment analysis of tweets related to Finland and NATO, using VADER (Hutto and Gilbert 2014) (2) Which entities have received the greatest attention in the discourse of the public? What sentiments were expressed towards the entities involved?
To answer this question, first, the entities which were talked about most need to be identified and second, the sentiment analysis needs to be performed, once again using VADER.
(3) What were the main topics used to discuss Finland joining NATO? During the months of discussions Finland joining NATO, did the public's perception change over time?
With regard to this question, the most popular topics and their development through time are investigated using the Latent Dirichlet Allocation model (LDA) (Blei and Lafferty 2006).

This study contributes to prior research by systematically analysing the Twitter communication of the public around the globe during Finland's application to join NATO. Consequently, the purpose of this article is to provide a more systematic and nuanced understanding of the public opinion and to identify its key components. By analysing the social media content within a longer period, between 12 May 2022 until 3 April 2023, this study should also provide an insight into discourse shifts. This is the first study so far to analyse the social media communication of the public during Finland's application to join NATO, applying computational text analysis tools to perform the analysis. By analysing these data, I hope to gain insights into the factors driving public opinion around NATO membership of Finland, as well as the potential implications of Finland's accession for regional security and stability.

paragraphs: The role of social media shaping political discourse and public opinion has been a subject of growing interest among policymakers and researchers. As Castells (2009) asserts, social media platforms have given rise to a new form of communication called mass self-communication, which enables individuals to both create and consume content on a large scale. Several studies show that social media can influence public opinion and even the outcome of elections (Bakshy, Messing, and Adamic 2015;Bond et al. 2012). In particular, Twitter is a popular political communication platform where users can express their opinions, share news, and engage in real-time conversations (Tumasjan et al. 2010). Jungherr (2016) and Enli (2017) point out that Twitter has become an important place for political debates, agendas and issue frames.

Social media platforms such as Twitter have enabled the democratization of information, providing tools for sharing content and updating in real time, enabling voices and perspectives to be heard from a variety of perspectives (Loader and Mercea 2011). However, this also raises concerns about the spread of misinformation, echo chambers and political polarization (Pariser 2011;Sunstein 2017). Analysing Twitter data can provide valuable insights into public sentiment, as it represents a snapshot of users' thoughts and opinions on a given topic. Furthermore, recent literature has highlighted important Russian trolls' phenomena in social media platforms, especially Twitter (Jensen 2018;Stewart, Arif, and Starbird 2018). These trolls are known for manipulating discussions, spreading false information, and creating divisions between users. Trolls often use tactics such as inflammatory remarks or controversial statements to provoke reactions and disturb conversations. Therefore, in analysing Twitter conversations, it is essential to consider the potential influence of these actors. This consideration is particularly important in discussions on sensitive issues such as the admission of Finland to NATO, where misinformation can have important consequences.

Researchers have employed various methods to examine Twitter data, such as content analysis (Small 2011), sentiment analysis (Heidbreder, Lange, and Reese 2021), and topic modelling (Nisch 2023). Social media platforms have changed the political discourse and public opinion formation in numerous ways. The analysis of Twitter data can help reveal public feelings and important discussions about this issue in Finland's NATO accession. By understanding the main themes and feelings, researchers can better understand the public's views on Finland's membership in NATO and the factors that contribute to its views. This in turn can inform policy makers and contribute to a broader literature on the role of social media in shaping political discourse.

paragraphs: Public opinion is a term that encapsulates the collective attitudes, beliefs, and viewpoints of a population regarding a particular issue, event, or policy (Allport 1937). Public opinion formation can be attributed to factors such as personal experiences, socialisation, exposure to the media, and group affiliation (Zaller 1992). Public opinion is often characterized by its complex nature, multidimensionality, and fluidity. It is influenced by a variety of factors and is changing over time with the emergence of new information, events, or perspectives (Converse 1964;Erikson, MacKuen, and Stimson 2002). In addition, public opinion can be divided into different levels of intensity, visibility and knowledge, resulting in the existence of a majority and a minority opinion within the population (Noelle-Neumann 1993). Several methods, including polls, surveys and focus groups, have been used to measure public opinion (Glynn et al. 2016). However, these traditional methods are confronted with challenges and limitations, which has led to the emergence of alternative methods such as social media data analysis (Tumasjan et al. 2010).

The formation of public opinion is a dynamic process whose development is influenced by several factors. The mass media play an important role in shaping public opinion through information, encapsulation of issues and establishment of agendas (Entman 2007;McCombs and Shaw 1972). Political elites and interest groups can influence the public opinion by publishing their viewpoints, advocating for specific policies, and mobilizing supporters (Page, Shapiro, and Dempsey 1987). Furthermore, social networks, cultural factors such as values and standards, and interpersonal communications contribute to the formation of public opinion (Inglehart and Welzel 2005).

Understanding public opinion is essential in democratic societies, as it reflects citizens' preferences and priorities, informs policy makers, and can influence policy results (Page and Shapiro 1983). A comprehensive understanding of public opinion ensures that policies respond more to the needs and interests of citizens and thus promote better governance and representation (Dahl 1989). Furthermore, public opinion can be a constraint for political elites by preventing them from pursuing policies that are unpopular or controversial (Jacobs and Shapiro 2000). Techniques such as sentiment analysis and topic modelling can be applied to Twitter data to study public opinion on specific topics, including Finland's NATO accession. sections.text:
Public opinion is a term that encapsulates the collective attitudes, beliefs, and viewpoints of a population regarding a particular issue, event, or policy (Allport 1937). Public opinion formation can be attributed to factors such as personal experiences, socialisation, exposure to the media, and group affiliation (Zaller 1992). Public opinion is often characterized by its complex nature, multidimensionality, and fluidity. It is influenced by a variety of factors and is changing over time with the emergence of new information, events, or perspectives (Converse 1964;Erikson, MacKuen, and Stimson 2002). In addition, public opinion can be divided into different levels of intensity, visibility and knowledge, resulting in the existence of a majority and a minority opinion within the population (Noelle-Neumann 1993). Several methods, including polls, surveys and focus groups, have been used to measure public opinion (Glynn et al. 2016). However, these traditional methods are confronted with challenges and limitations, which has led to the emergence of alternative methods such as social media data analysis (Tumasjan et al. 2010).

The formation of public opinion is a dynamic process whose development is influenced by several factors. The mass media play an important role in shaping public opinion through information, encapsulation of issues and establishment of agendas (Entman 2007;McCombs and Shaw 1972). Political elites and interest groups can influence the public opinion by publishing their viewpoints, advocating for specific policies, and mobilizing supporters (Page, Shapiro, and Dempsey 1987). Furthermore, social networks, cultural factors such as values and standards, and interpersonal communications contribute to the formation of public opinion (Inglehart and Welzel 2005).

Understanding public opinion is essential in democratic societies, as it reflects citizens' preferences and priorities, informs policy makers, and can influence policy results (Page and Shapiro 1983). A comprehensive understanding of public opinion ensures that policies respond more to the needs and interests of citizens and thus promote better governance and representation (Dahl 1989). Furthermore, public opinion can be a constraint for political elites by preventing them from pursuing policies that are unpopular or controversial (Jacobs and Shapiro 2000). This makes the assessment of public opinion particularly important in the context of Finland's accession to NATO. The insights from public opinion can inform policy makers of their public opinion on this issue, which can influence policy-making, national security, and regional stability.

Social media platforms such as Twitter have become a valuable source of real-time and unfiltered public opinion data (Bruns and Burgess 2011). Techniques such as sentiment analysis and topic modelling can be applied to Twitter data to study public opinion on specific topics, including Finland's NATO accession. Further, the research of Tumasjan et al. Nisch - 2024 - Public opinion about Finland joining NATO analysing Twitter posts by performing natural language pr
paragraphs: This study uses tweets with the hashtags #NATO and #Finland from 12 May 2022 until 3 April 2023 to examine public perception and opinions around the Globe regarding Finland's NATO membership. The two mentioned hashtags were used to capture focused conversations directly pertaining to Finland's NATO membership. The raw data was collected on 6 April 2023 by using the R package rtweet (Kearney et al. 2023). The acquisition culminated in a total of 34,884 tweets. Each tweet encompassed textual content, creation metadata, and the respective user's Twitter user name.
The following methodology was employed for the processing and examination of the Twitter dataset, utilizing R and Python programming languages:
(1) Raw data extraction: Post data accumulation in R, regular expressions were employed, the textual data was exported as a spreadsheet in Excel format.

(2) Textual pre-processing: he retrieved tweets underwent pre-processing via Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques in order to clean up the content, eliminate irrelevant data, and make them processable.

(3) Sentiment Analysis: VADER (Valence Aware Dictionary and sEntiment Reasoner) (Hutto and Gilbert 2014) was applied to perform sentiment analysis on the amassed tweets, facilitating the identification and extraction of attitudes, emotions, evaluative judgments, and opinions. (4) Topic Extraction: Following appropriate pre-processing, Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) was employed to discern the predominant topics within the tweet corpus and track their evolution over time.

paragraphs: A tweet constitutes a message disseminated on the Twitter platform, characterized by a textual content limited to 280 characters. The inclusion of a hyperlink reduces the character limit to 257. Raw tweets contain redundant information and exhibit a highly unstructured nature. R, Python, and NLP approaches were utilised across several pre-processing stages to transform the raw data into a format amenable to sentiment and topic analyses:

(1) R-based Pre-processing: Subsequent to data acquisition, regular expressions were applied to eliminate mentions, URLs, hashtags, HTML characters (e.g. substituting '&' with 'and', removing tags such as
,

, etc.), excising unnecessary line breaks, discarding special characters and punctuation marks (excluding exclamation points as VADER employs them in sentiment analysis), and removing numerals and dates.

(2) Translation: The Google Translate API ('googletrans' tool) was utilized to translate any language tweets' text content into English. (Google LLC 2023). (3) Data Refinement for LDA: As second part a higher quality dataset is needed for the LDA.

Duplicate tweets were removed, retaining only unique entries. The vocabulary was pruned by removing stop words, and terms irrelevant to the topic (like 'as', 'from', 'would', etc.), followed by the creation of a bigram model. Further enhancements could be achieved through lemmatisation and tokenisation to improve the model's results (Ghosh and Gunning 2019).

The text was then tokenised using the Gensim library (Rehurek and Sojka 2010) to ensure that every word appears in a consistent format. Lastly, the spaCy library within the Natural Language Toolkit (NLTK) (Bird, Klein, and Loper 2009) was employed for lemmatisation.

sections.paper_id: Nisch - 2024 - Public opinion about Finland joining NATO analysing Twitter posts by performing natural language pr
paragraphs: Humans associate words, phrases, and sentences with emotions. Sentiment analysis involves the utilization of computational techniques to extract and scrutinize the emotions conveyed in a document. The Valence Aware Dictionary and Sentiment Reasoner (VADER) model was employed in the current study to interpret sentiments, opinions, evaluations, and emotions about Finland's application to join NATO within the collected tweets. Vader, a pre-trained sentiment lexicon and rule-based sentiment analysis tool, was initially introduced by Hutto and Gilbert in 2014 (Hutto and Gilbert 2014) with a focus on social media texts. The model works exceedingly well in microblogging contexts where the short text data is a complex mix of a variety of features and a sentence may contain multiple sentiments simultaneously. Accessible as a 'vaderSentiment' module within the Python NLTK library, VADER Sentiment Analysis can be directly applied to analyse unlabelled text. Advantages of VADER over traditional sentiment analysis approaches include:

• There is no need for training data, as VADER is built upon a crowd validated, standard sentiment lexicon, replete with sentiment intensity measures, tailored for social media platforms like Twitter.

• It has been demonstrated to outperform individual human raters and perform as well as (and in most cases, better than) other widely regarded sentiment analysis tools when determining the sentiment of tweets (Hutto and Gilbert 2014).
• Rapid computational performance without significant speed performance trade-offs.
• Vader was already used in several similar studies before (Stracqualursi and Agati 2022, Rustam et al. 2021, Moussa, Mohamed and Haggag 2020;Monselise et al. 2021).

VADER conducts sentiment analysis based on its rules, employing various lexical features including capitalisation, punctuation, degree modifiers, the contrastive conjunction 'but', and the negation flipping tri-gram.

The sentiment score (Ss) of a phrase is determined by summing the lexical rates of each word that is included in the VADER dictionary. After appropriate normalization, VADER generates a 'compound' Ss ranging from -1 to 1, with -1 being the most negative and 1 the most positive. Threshold values can be applied to classify tweets as negative, neutral, or positive once the Ss is known. In this study, the VADER model returns a sentiment score, which is utilised to apply the designated thresholds for classifying each tweet's polarity as positive, neutral, or negative (refer to Table 1).

paragraphs: The objective of the unsupervised machine learning technique, referred to as 'Topic Modelling', is to find the topics that most accurately embody the content of a document collection. It is a text mining approach that identifies the topics of texts from a large corpus of previously gathered documents (Blei, Ng, and Jordan 2003). Among the most prevalent topic modelling methods is the Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) model, a three level hierarchical Bayesian probabilistic model designed to represent a corpus. The concept of LDA is that each document contributes to a topic, and that the topic is characterised as a word distribution (Lee et al. 2018). The process of constructing documents within a corpus, particularly in the context of LDA models, proceeds as follows:
(1) θ is sampled from a Dirichlet prior mixture of t topics, which is parameterised by α.
(2) Following that, a topic t n is then sampled from a multinomial distribution, p θ j α ð Þ which is the document topic distribution and models p t n ¼ i j θ ð Þ:

(3) The number of topics is set to t ¼ 1 . . . ; T, and the distribution of words with t topics is described by, ϕ, which is also a multinomial distribution whose hyper-parameter β follows the Dirichlet distribution. (4) Through the topic t n , a word, w n , is then sampled via the multinomial distribution p w j z n : β ð Þ.
A document -for example, in our specific case, tweet 0 k 0 -containing words is described as:

Dynamic topic modelling enables the examination of text topics' evolution over time. The dataset corpus used for this research spans from 12 May 2022 to 3 April 2023 and is analysed using the model provided by gensim (Rehurek and Sojka 2010). It can be observed that the content of tweets evolves over time, suggesting that the subject matter is not a static corpus. The Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) can be used on topics aggregated over time, while a state space model manages topic transitions from one epoch to another. As an extra dimension, a gaussian probabilistic model is used to determine the posterior probability of the topics that are developing over time.

Identifying the right number of topics is a crucial challenge in LDA. Coherence scores were suggested by Röder, Both, and Hinneburg (2015) as a means to assess the efficacy of each topic model. Topic coherence specifically measures the consistency between topics inferred by a model. For the coherence measure, Cv was used, which is a measure based on a sliding window that employs cosine similarity and normalised point wise mutual information (NPMI). These values reflect the extent to which a topic's words 'make sense' and are designed to approximate the relative score that a human would probably give it.

These ratings indicate the degree of coherence among the 'top' words within a specific topic. Additionally, the distribution of the principal component analysis (PCA), is considered, enabling the visualization of topic models within a two dimensional spatial framework. It is preferable to have a uniform distribution, which is perceived as having a high degree of independence for each topic. A quality model is characterised by higher coherence and an average distribution in the primary analysis, as demonstrated by the pyLDAvis (Sievert and Shirley 2014).

The objective of the unsupervised machine learning technique, referred to as 'Topic Modelling', is to find the topics that most accurately embody the content of a document collection. It is a text mining approach that identifies the topics of texts from a large corpus of previously gathered documents (Blei, Ng, and Jordan 2003). Among the most prevalent topic modelling methods is the Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) model, a three level hierarchical Bayesian probabilistic model designed to represent a corpus. The concept of LDA is that each document contributes to a topic, and that the topic is characterised as a word distribution (Lee et al. 2018). The process of constructing documents within a corpus, particularly in the context of LDA models, proceeds as follows:

(1) θ is sampled from a Dirichlet prior mixture of t topics, which is parameterised by α.

(2) Following that, a topic t n is then sampled from a multinomial distribution, p θ j α ð Þ which is the document topic distribution and models p t n ¼ i j θ ð Þ:

(3) The number of topics is set to t ¼ 1 . . . ; T, and the distribution of words with t topics is described by, ϕ, which is also a multinomial distribution whose hyper-parameter β follows the Dirichlet distribution. (4) Through the topic t n , a word, w n , is then sampled via the multinomial distribution p w j z n : β ð Þ.

A document -for example, in our specific case, tweet 0 k 0 -containing words is described as:

Dynamic topic modelling enables the examination of text topics' evolution over time. The dataset corpus used for this research spans from 12 May 2022 to 3 April 2023 and is analysed using the model provided by gensim (Rehurek and Sojka 2010). It can be observed that the content of tweets evolves over time, suggesting that the subject matter is not a static corpus. The Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) can be used on topics aggregated over time, while a state space model manages topic transitions from one epoch to another. As an extra dimension, a gaussian probabilistic model is used to determine the posterior probability of the topics that are developing over time.

Identifying the right number of topics is a crucial challenge in LDA. Coherence scores were suggested by Röder, Both, and Hinneburg (2015) as a means to assess the efficacy of each topic model. Topic coherence specifically measures the consistency between topics inferred by a model. For the coherence measure, Cv was used, which is a measure based on a sliding window that employs cosine similarity and normalised point wise mutual information (NPMI). These values reflect the extent to which a topic's words 'make sense' and are designed to approximate the relative score that a human would probably give it.

These ratings indicate the degree of coherence among the 'top' words within a specific topic. Additionally, the distribution of the principal component analysis (PCA), is considered, enabling the visualization of topic models within a two dimensional spatial framework. It is preferable to have a uniform distribution, which is perceived as having a high degree of independence for each topic. A quality model is characterised by higher coherence and an average distribution in the primary analysis, as demonstrated by the pyLDAvis (Sievert and Shirley 2014).

The combination of quantitative and qualitative analyses thus provides a comprehensive understanding of the public opinion towards the Finnish application to NATO on Twitter.

paragraphs: In order to examine the public sentiment regarding Finland's potential accession to NATO as expressed on Twitter, the LDA model was employed on a pre-processed corpus, incorporating solely the subsequent part of speech elements: nouns, adjectives, verbs, and adverbs.

Table 3. Example tweets with polarity and the sentiment score. As previously mentioned, Cv was used as a measure of topic coherence in order to determine the appropriate number of topics, reflecting the coherence between topics derived from the model (Röder, Both, and Hinneburg 2015). Moreover, the distribution of the principal component analysis (PCA) was considered, as it represents the topic models within a two-dimensional spatial context. A uniform distribution is preferred, as it is indicative of a high degree of independence for each individual topic. The attainment of a successful model is contingent upon a high degree of coherence and an average distribution in the principal component analysis, as exhibited by the pyLDAvis (Sievert and Shirley 2014).

The LDA models were initiated with a range of topic numbers (k) from 2 to 25, with the purpose of ascertaining the coherence of the model. The coherence score achieved its apex at 3, 6, and 9 topic numbers, as illustrated in Figure 4. By selecting 6 or 9 topics, this would lead to a non-uniform distribution on the primer component analysis (PCA), which would result in a low degree of independence for each topic (see the results of the LDAvis interactive map in Figure 5). By choosing the three topics, the model intersects with no other topics, the whole word space is well summarised, and the topics are independent (Syed and Spruit 2017).

The LDA analysis result, containing the top 12 words, is shown in Table 5. The words are listed in order of their frequency. The first topic takes up 42% of total tokens and includes words such as 'membership', 'alliance', 'security', and 'application'. Based on this, it was inferred that the individuals articulated the alliances Finland is entering and the associated enhancement of security it engenders. The second topic had 35% of the total tokens, including the words 'war', 'Russian', 'threat', 'Ukraine', and 'nuclear', indicating people sharing concerns and the possible motives of Finland joining NATO. The last topic, with 23% of the tokens, included words like 'invasion', 'news', and 'expansion' but also had positive words like 'good', 'great', and 'strong', all of which can be interpreted to mean that the population expressed their opinions about Finland joining NATO.
The LDA model produced three key topics that persisted throughout:
• Topic 1: Alliances creation by Finland joining NATO • Topic 2: Concerns and motives of Finland joining NATO • Topic 3: Opinions about Finland joining NATO.

Figure 6 shows the dynamic change in the occurrence of each topic during the observed period to identify a shift in the discourse. It is worth noting that the alliance topic dominated in the first third and the last third of observation. In between September and January, when the conversation about Finland joining NATO was not that present, a discourse shift happened and the concerns dominated. The public wrote the least about the 'opinions' Finland joining NATO. Figure 7 presents the results of the sentiment analysis conducted on the previously identified topics, namely 'Alliances', 'Opinions', and 'Concerns'. The analysis reveals that the sentiments expressed within the 'Alliances' and 'Opinions' topics predominantly exhibit positive sentiment, whereas the 'Concerns' topic demonstrates a predominantly negative sentiment. This distinction underscores the varying emotional responses to different aspects of the discourse surrounding Finland's potential NATO accession.

sections.paper_id: Nisch - 2024 - Public opinion about Finland joining NATO analysing Twitter posts by performing natural language pr
paragraphs: With Twitter becoming a platform for information, politics, and social exchange, it is clear that social media can be used as a powerful tool of mass communication. With a wide range of daily conversations, discussions, and even political debates, governments and politicians can use Twitter to get insights of the public opinion. This trend has been observed through various studies looking at how people interact with their elected representatives using the platform in order to express their feelings and opinions. There is currently limited information available about Finland's application journey to join NATO. This makes investigating public opinion on social media regarding Finland's potential NATO membership crucial, as it provides insights into the collective sentiment and concerns of the population, which may inform policy decisions and help gauge the potential impact of such a strategic move on domestic politics and society. Furthermore, this is the first study that uses Twitter to identify opinions of the public about the topic of Finland joining NATO during their announcement until Finland was admitted. Rahkonen (2017) analysed opinion polls in Finland and how NATO topics are covered there by the media, but this was done over 5 years ago, before events like the invasion of Ukraine, and focused on traditional media and polls. The answers to the three main questions from the introduction related to the public opinion on Finland joining NATO are as follows:
(1) The analysis reveals that the overall sentiment of the public expressed on Twitter regarding Finland joining NATO leans moderately positive.

The moderately positive sentiment expressed on Twitter regarding Finland's potential NATO membership can be interpreted as an indication of the public's cautious confidence in the alliance and their belief that Finland's membership would yield benefits for the general public. This sentiment may be influenced by various factors, including Finland's security concerns, the evolving geopolitical landscape, and the perceived role of NATO in upholding regional stability. Consequently, it suggests that public opinion regarding Finland's NATO membership leans towards support, reflecting an optimistic perspective on the potential consequences of this strategic choice. When comparing this sentiment to the level of support for joining NATO within the Finnish population from the YLE poll, the moderately positive sentiment observed on Twitter differ with a broader trend of favourable attitudes towards NATO membership. (2) The entities that received the greatest attention in the Twitter discourse include Sweden, Turkey, Russia, Ukraine, Stoltenberg, Putin, Erdogan, and Europe. The sentiment analysis reveals that positive sentiment is primarily associated with Sweden, Turkey, and Stoltenberg, while negative sentiment is predominantly linked to Russia, Ukraine, Putin, Erdogan, and Europe.

The importance of these entities in Twitter discussions highlights their influence on the formation of public opinion on Finland's NATO accession. The positive sentiments towards Sweden and Stoltenberg may be due to the perception of their support for Finland's NATO ambitions or the advantages they see in their participation in the alliance. On the contrary, the negative sentiment towards Russia, Ukraine, and Putin may stem from their connection to the geopolitical context that has led to Finland's application to NATO. Interestingly, Turkey received a majority of positive remarks, despite the fact that Erdogan was mainly mentioned in a negative context. This suggests that the public differentiated between Erdogan's opposition to NATO entry and the broader sentiments towards the Turkish people, recognizing that the Turkish population should not be held responsible for the negative perceptions associated with Erdogan.

(3) The main topics used to discuss Finland joining NATO, as identified by the LDA analysis, include alliances creation, concerns and motives, and opinions about Finland joining NATO. Over the months of discussion, public perception did indeed change, as evidenced by the dynamic shifts in topic appearance. Notably, concerns dominated the conversation when Finland's NATO application was less prominent, while alliances and opinions were more prevalent during the initial and final stages of the observation period.

The identified topics reflect the main aspects of the public debate on Finland's NATO membership on Twitter. The focus on the establishment of alliances demonstrates interest in the formation of strategic alliances and in the potential benefits and consequences of Finland's accession to NATO. Concerns and motives highlight the fears and underlying motives behind the public sentiment, reflecting the complexities and different perspectives of the problem. Opinions on Finland's accession to NATO suggest an active commitment and expression of individual views in public discussions. The dynamic shifts in topic appearance over time indicate a changing public perception in response to the evolving geopolitical context and the progress of Finland's NATO application. The dominance of concerns when the application was less prominent suggests that public attention intensified as the topic gained prominence. The prevalence of alliances and opinions during the initial and final stages of the observation period suggests heightened interest and engagement during crucial stages of the application process.

In summary, the analysis conducted in this study provides valuable insights into the public sentiment, key entities, and main topics discussed on Twitter regarding Finland's potential NATO accession. By examining the sentiment and topic dynamics over time, this study contributes to a deeper understanding of the public's perception and the factors shaping discourse on this important geopolitical issue.

The findings of this study are supported by various studies in the literature and therefore contributes to the existing body of knowledge and offers valuable insights into the public's perception of this important geopolitical issue. Specifically, the findings of this study can be supported by other studies in the following ways:

• Sentiment analysis: Numerous studies have also employed sentiment analysis to understand the public opinion on social media platforms such as Twitter (Tumasjan et al. 2010;Bollen, Mao, andZeng 2011, Stracqualursi andAgati 2022). These studies confirm the effectiveness of sentiment analysis in capturing public and general emotions, strengthening the conclusions of moderately positive emotions surrounding Finland's accession to NATO in this study. • Importance of key entities: The key entities identified in this study as receiving the most attention align with findings from other research exploring geopolitical discourse and public opinion (Arter 2023;Raunio and Tiilikainen 2003). The significant roles these entities play in shaping the geopolitical landscape and influencing public perception of Finland's NATO application support their prominence in the current study. • Topic modelling: Several studies have employed topic modelling techniques, such as LDA, to analyse public discourse on social media (Blei, Ng, and Jordan 2003;DiMaggio, Nag, andBlei 2013, Stracqualursi andAgati 2022). These studies show that topic modelling is effective in revealing key topics and subjects discussed by the public, supporting the findings of this research on the three main topics (alliances creation, concerns and motives, and opinions about Finland joining NATO). • Public perception dynamics: Research on the dynamics of public opinion has established that public perception can evolve over time in response to contextual shifts and events (Burstein 2003;Inglehart and Welzel 2005;Wlezien 2004). These studies contribute to the findings of the present study that public perception changed during the months of discussions surrounding Finland's NATO application, with concerns dominating the conversation during certain periods while alliances and opinions were more prevalent during others.

The findings of this study are supported by various studies in the literature and therefore contributes to the existing body of knowledge and offers valuable insights into the public's perception of this important geopolitical issue.

The results of this research can provide valuable insights that can help various stakeholders and contribute to a deeper understanding of public opinion in the context of Finland's NATO accession. These insights are particularly relevant to policy makers and government officials, who can benefit from a deeper understanding of public sentiment and the main issues of concern related to Finland's accession to NATO. This information enables them to respond more effectively to public concerns, improve communication and make informed decisions in accordance with public interests. In addition, public opinion analysis on Finland's accession to NATO provides important information for NATO and its Member States. This information can guide their diplomatic strategies, communication efforts and decisions on future enlargements or cooperation with non-member States. Furthermore, it contributes to existing literature on the role of public opinion, geopolitics and social media in forming discourse. Consequently, political analysts and researchers can use these conclusions as a reference point for future research and further research into the dynamics of public opinion in the context of geopolitical events. The Media and journalists can also benefit from the knowledge gained through this research, as it can inform their media coverage and journalistic reporting on Finland's NATO accession. Understanding the public opinions, concerns and views will enable journalists to create more accurate and balanced stories reflecting public views. Finally, through these conclusions, the general public can better understand the general public's opinion on Finland's accession to NATO. This knowledge can lead to more informed discussions and debates among citizens and promote more nuanced and informed civic discussions.

Apart from what it may contribute, this study also has several limitations. First, the focus was solely on Twitter material. However, depending on their preferences, language, and interests, users may be spread over many social networking sites. However, the techniques used in this study may be applied to a variety of social media platforms. sections.text:
With Twitter becoming a platform for information, politics, and social exchange, it is clear that social media can be used as a powerful tool of mass communication. With a wide range of daily conversations, discussions, and even political debates, governments and politicians can use Twitter to get insights of the public opinion. This trend has been observed through various studies looking at how people interact with their elected representatives using the platform in order to express their feelings and opinions. There is currently limited information available about Finland's application journey to join NATO. This makes investigating public opinion on social media regarding Finland's potential NATO membership crucial, as it provides insights into the collective sentiment and concerns of the population, which may inform policy decisions and help gauge the potential impact of such a strategic move on domestic politics and society. Furthermore, this is the first study that uses Twitter to identify opinions of the public about the topic of Finland joining NATO during their announcement until Finland was admitted. Rahkonen (2017) analysed opinion polls in Finland and how NATO topics are covered there by the media, but this was done over 5 years ago, before events like the invasion of Ukraine, and focused on traditional media and polls. The answers to the three main questions from the introduction related to the public opinion on Finland joining NATO are as follows:

(1) The analysis reveals that the overall sentiment of the public expressed on Twitter regarding Finland joining NATO leans moderately positive.

The moderately positive sentiment expressed on Twitter regarding Finland's potential NATO membership can be interpreted as an indication of the public's cautious confidence in the alliance and their belief that Finland's membership would yield benefits for the general public. This sentiment may be influenced by various factors, including Finland's security concerns, the evolving geopolitical landscape, and the perceived role of NATO in upholding regional stability. Consequently, it suggests that public opinion regarding Finland's NATO membership leans towards support, reflecting an optimistic perspective on the potential consequences of this strategic choice. When comparing this sentiment to the level of support for joining NATO within the Finnish population from the YLE poll, the moderately positive sentiment observed on Twitter differ with a broader trend of favourable attitudes towards NATO membership. (2) The entities that received the greatest attention in the Twitter discourse include Sweden, Turkey, Russia, Ukraine, Stoltenberg, Putin, Erdogan, and Europe. The sentiment analysis reveals that positive sentiment is primarily associated with Sweden, Turkey, and Stoltenberg, while negative sentiment is predominantly linked to Russia, Ukraine, Putin, Erdogan, and Europe.

The importance of these entities in Twitter discussions highlights their influence on the formation of public opinion on Finland's NATO accession. The positive sentiments towards Sweden and Stoltenberg may be due to the perception of their support for Finland's NATO ambitions or the advantages they see in their participation in the alliance. On the contrary, the negative sentiment towards Russia, Ukraine, and Putin may stem from their connection to the geopolitical context that has led to Finland's application to NATO. Interestingly, Turkey received a majority of positive remarks, despite the fact that Erdogan was mainly mentioned in a negative context. This suggests that the public differentiated between Erdogan's opposition to NATO entry and the broader sentiments towards the Turkish people, recognizing that the Turkish population should not be held responsible for the negative perceptions associated with Erdogan.

(3) The main topics used to discuss Finland joining NATO, as identified by the LDA analysis, include alliances creation, concerns and motives, and opinions about Finland joining NATO. Over the months of discussion, public perception did indeed change, as evidenced by the dynamic shifts in topic appearance. Notably, concerns dominated the conversation when Finland's NATO application was less prominent, while alliances and opinions were more prevalent during the initial and final stages of the observation period.

The identified topics reflect the main aspects of the public debate on Finland's NATO membership on Twitter. The focus on the establishment of alliances demonstrates interest in the formation of strategic alliances and in the potential benefits and consequences of Finland's accession to NATO. Concerns and motives highlight the fears and underlying motives behind the public sentiment, reflecting the complexities and different perspectives of the problem. Opinions on Finland's accession to NATO suggest an active commitment and expression of individual views in public discussions. The dynamic shifts in topic appearance over time indicate a changing public perception in response to the evolving geopolitical context and the progress of Finland's NATO application. The dominance of concerns when the application was less prominent suggests that public attention intensified as the topic gained prominence. The prevalence of alliances and opinions during the initial and final stages of the observation period suggests heightened interest and engagement during crucial stages of the application process.

In summary, the analysis conducted in this study provides valuable insights into the public sentiment, key entities, and main topics discussed on Twitter regarding Finland's potential NATO accession. By examining the sentiment and topic dynamics over time, this study contributes to a deeper understanding of the public's perception and the factors shaping discourse on this important geopolitical issue.

The findings of this study are supported by various studies in the literature and therefore contributes to the existing body of knowledge and offers valuable insights into the public's perception of this important geopolitical issue. Specifically, the findings of this study can be supported by other studies in the following ways:

• Sentiment analysis: Numerous studies have also employed sentiment analysis to understand the public opinion on social media platforms such as Twitter (Tumasjan et al. 2010;Bollen, Mao, andZeng 2011, Stracqualursi andAgati 2022). These studies confirm the effectiveness of sentiment analysis in capturing public and general emotions, strengthening the conclusions of moderately positive emotions surrounding Finland's accession to NATO in this study. • Importance of key entities: The key entities identified in this study as receiving the most attention align with findings from other research exploring geopolitical discourse and public opinion (Arter 2023;Raunio and Tiilikainen 2003). The significant roles these entities play in shaping the geopolitical landscape and influencing public perception of Finland's NATO application support their prominence in the current study. • Topic modelling: Several studies have employed topic modelling techniques, such as LDA, to analyse public discourse on social media (Blei, Ng, and Jordan 2003;DiMaggio, Nag, andBlei 2013, Stracqualursi andAgati 2022). These studies show that topic modelling is effective in revealing key topics and subjects discussed by the public, supporting the findings of this research on the three main topics (alliances creation, concerns and motives, and opinions about Finland joining NATO). • Public perception dynamics: Research on the dynamics of public opinion has established that public perception can evolve over time in response to contextual shifts and events (Burstein 2003;Inglehart and Welzel 2005;Wlezien 2004). These studies contribute to the findings of the present study that public perception changed during the months of discussions surrounding Finland's NATO application, with concerns dominating the conversation during certain periods while alliances and opinions were more prevalent during others.

The findings of this study are supported by various studies in the literature and therefore contributes to the existing body of knowledge and offers valuable insights into the public's perception of this important geopolitical issue.

The results of this research can provide valuable insights that can help various stakeholders and contribute to a deeper understanding of public opinion in the context of Finland's NATO accession. These insights are particularly relevant to policy makers and government officials, who can benefit from a deeper understanding of public sentiment and the main issues of concern related to Finland's accession to NATO. This information enables them to respond more effectively to public concerns, improve communication and make informed decisions in accordance with public interests. In addition, public opinion analysis on Finland's accession to NATO provides important information for NATO and its Member States. This information can guide their diplomatic strategies, communication efforts and decisions on future enlargements or cooperation with non-member States. Furthermore, it contributes to existing literature on the role of public opinion, geopolitics and social media in forming discourse. Consequently, political analysts and researchers can use these conclusions as a reference point for future research and further research into the dynamics of public opinion in the context of geopolitical events. The Media and journalists can also benefit from the knowledge gained through this research, as it can inform their media coverage and journalistic reporting on Finland's NATO accession. Understanding the public opinions, concerns and views will enable journalists to create more accurate and balanced stories reflecting public views. Finally, through these conclusions, the general public can better understand the general public's opinion on Finland's accession to NATO. This knowledge can lead to more informed discussions and debates among citizens and promote more nuanced and informed civic discussions.
